Metadata.Coram: Darco Water Technologies Ltd v Thye Kim Meng [2022] SGHC 49
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Darco Water Technologies Ltd

Facts.Text: Darco Water Technologies Ltd was incorporated on 13 October 2001.
Darco Water Technologies Ltd was listed on the main board of the Singapore Exchange on 7 May 2008.
Darco Water Technologies Ltd is in the business of water and waste-water treatment solutions and is part of the Darco Group of companies.

The Darco Group is governed by a Group Charter which sets out principles for management and governance of each company in the group, including internal guidelines for material contracts and investments exceeding specified amounts.
At the material time, Darco Water Technologies Ltd had a board of directors referred to as the Board.
Darco Water Technologies Ltd is the holding company of Darco Water System Sdn Bhd, a wholly owned Malaysian-incorporated subsidiary.
Darco Water System Sdn Bhd was tasked to carry out the Solar Project in Vietnam.

In October 2017, the defendant contacted Mr Dao regarding a business opportunity in Can Giuoc District, Vietnam involving the plaintiff's acquisition of 90% of the issued equity interest in Can Giuoc Water Works Limited from CA Trading Co Ltd.

On 7 November 2017, representatives of Darco Water Technologies Ltd, including Thye Kim Meng and Heather Tan, met in Singapore with consultant Dinh Minh Dao to discuss the proposed Water Project involving Can Giuoc Water Works Limited.

On 11 December 2017, Thye Kim Meng sent Heather Tan draft terms of a framework agreement for the Water Project to be signed between Darco Water Technologies Ltd and CA Trading Co Ltd, and Heather Tan formalised the agreement.

On 14 December 2017, the Framework Agreement was signed between Darco Water Technologies Ltd and CA Trading Co Ltd. Under cl 5, the US$1 million deposit was stated to be a refundable deposit recoverable upon the discovery of any negative, unfavourable or adverse findings of a technical or financial nature that rendered the plaintiff unable to proceed with entry into a sale and purchase agreement for the acquisition of the 90% equity interest in Canwaco.
On 14 December 2017, pursuant to the Framework Agreement, Mr Teh remitted US$1 million to CA Trading Co Ltd as the Deposit.
On 20 April 2018, Mazars LLP was engaged to carry out a financial due diligence exercise on the Water Project relating to the potential acquisition of equity in Can Giuoc Water Works Limited.

In October 2018, Mr Dao informed the defendant that he had a joint venture with a Vietnamese company via a corporate entity known as Con Dao Green Energy to construct and operate a solar power plant on Dat Doc beach on one of the Con Dao islands. This was the Solar Project and it involved the plaintiff's potential acquisition of CDGE.
The defendant then directed Mr Zach Thye to work on the Solar Project.
In October 2018, director Thye Kim Meng instructed Darco Water System Sdn Bhd managing director Thye Ze Pin to work on the Solar Project.
On 30 October 2018, a letter of intent was entered into by DWS and CDGE. It contained a payment schedule for the payment of US$1m over five tranches with the requisite conditions.

On 30 November 2018, DWS and CDGE signed a document titled 'Request for Advancement' pursuant to the 30 October 2018 LOI. It set out a six-tranche payment schedule for an advance sum of US$1m and conditions for each tranche, including provision of legal and project documents and completion of due diligence to the satisfaction of the Purchaser.
On 30 November 2018, the first payment of US$200,000 was made to Mr Dao.

On 30 November 2018, Darco Water Technologies Ltd, on behalf of Darco Water System Sdn Bhd, made a first payment of US$200,000 to consultant Dinh Minh Dao pursuant to the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter for the Solar Project.

On 28 December 2018, finance director Heather Tan emailed consultant Dinh Minh Dao requesting additional data regarding the salinity of the water source for the Water Project, copying director Thye Kim Meng on the correspondence.
On 10 January 2019, the second payment of US$300,000 was made to Mr Dao.
On 25 February 2019, at an audit committee meeting, the defendant explained that the due diligence process for the Water Project was ongoing.
On 15 February 2019, the third payment of US$100,000 was made to Mr Dao.

Three payments totalling US$600,000 were made to Mr Dao under the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter. By that letter, the payments were refundable under cl 2 if any one or more of the conditions outlined therein were not fulfilled.
For the Solar Project, the defendant had approved an inter-company loan of US$1m from the plaintiff to DWS.

By the time of the trial in November 2021, Darco Water Technologies Ltd had not fully recovered the US$1,000,000 deposit paid to CA Trading Co Ltd for the Water Project and the US$600,000 paid to consultant Dinh Minh Dao for the Solar Project.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company may hold its former CEO liable for the non-recovery of deposits and advance payments disbursed for overseas infrastructure projects that ultimately failed to materialize.
Party_Details.Rule: A company may bring an action for breach of fiduciary duties and negligence against a director who executes material contracts or authorizes substantial payments without obtaining required board approval, or who recklessly fails to ensure that requisite due diligence conditions are met before disbursing corporate funds. However, courts will not second-guess bona fide commercial decisions that simply turn out poorly, provided the director acted within their authority, relied reasonably on delegated management, and possessed a rational commercial basis for the transaction.
Party_Details.Application: Darco Water Technologies Ltd (DWTL) sought to expand into Vietnam. The CEO, Thye Kim Meng, caused DWTL to enter into a Framework Agreement for a 'Water Project,' executing a US$1,000,000 refundable deposit before financial due diligence by Mazars LLP was completed. Simultaneously, he approved an inter-company loan of US$1,000,000 to a subsidiary (DWS) for a 'Solar Project.' Through DWS, and pursuant to an Advancement Letter, three payments totaling US$600,000 were disbursed to a consultant, Mr. Dao. Neither project progressed to a final sale and purchase agreement, and the advanced funds were not recovered. DWTL alleges the CEO breached his duties by bypassing the Board of Directors, ignoring internal financial thresholds set by the Group Charter, and recklessly disbursing funds without satisfying the due diligence conditions precedent. The legal tension centers on whether these unrecovered multi-million dollar disbursements were the result of the CEO’s rogue, negligent mismanagement or standard, authorized commercial risk-taking that merely ended in a bad investment.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the company's claims entirely, finding no breach of fiduciary duty or negligence by the former CEO. First, the court interpreted the internal Group Charter and concluded that formal Board approval was only required for definitive acquisition contracts exceeding S$20 million. The Framework Agreement and the Advancement Letter were preliminary, exploratory agreements designed to secure exclusivity and facilitate due diligence, not final acquisitions; therefore, the CEO possessed the authority to execute them and authorize the corresponding deposits without Board approval. Second, the court found the quantum of the US$1 million Water Project deposit commercially reasonable given the overall valuation of the target assets. Third, regarding due diligence, the court determined that the CEO had properly delegated the Mazars LLP financial review to the Investment Committee and finance personnel. He was not directly involved in the granular correspondence and was entitled to rely on his subordinates. Because the due diligence was genuinely ongoing and unresolved issues (like water salinity) were being actively managed, there was no reckless failure that required the CEO to instantly demand a refund. Regarding the Solar Project, the subsidiary (DWS) was the primary executing entity. The CEO's role was limited to authorizing the inter-company funding. He reasonably relied on the subsidiary's managing director to ensure the tranche conditions were met before disbursement. Finally, the company's eventual withdrawal from the projects was driven by evolving commercial reasons long after the CEO's departure, breaking any causal link between his initial authorizations and the ultimate non-recovery of the funds. The court emphasized that it will not use hindsight to penalize directors for honest commercial decisions that result in financial loss.

Metadata.Coram: Darco Water Technologies Ltd v Thye Kim Meng [2022] SGHC 49
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Thye Kim Meng

Facts.Text: Thye Kim Meng is the founder of Darco Water Technologies Ltd and was a director of the company from 13 October 2001.

At the material time concerning the Water Project and Solar Project, Thye Kim Meng was the managing director and chief executive officer of Darco Water Technologies Ltd and served as executive chairman of its Board.

In October 2017, Thye Kim Meng contacted consultant Dinh Minh Dao regarding a business opportunity in Can Giuoc District, Vietnam involving a potential acquisition of 90% of the issued equity interest in Can Giuoc Water Works Limited from CA Trading Co Ltd.

On 7 November 2017, Thye Kim Meng attended a meeting in Singapore with finance director Heather Tan and consultant Dinh Minh Dao to discuss the proposed Water Project involving Can Giuoc Water Works Limited.
On 11 December 2017, Thye Kim Meng sent Heather Tan draft terms of a framework agreement for the Water Project to be signed between Darco Water Technologies Ltd and CA Trading Co Ltd.

On 14 December 2017, acting in his capacity as managing director and chief executive officer, Thye Kim Meng caused Darco Water Technologies Ltd to enter into a Framework Agreement with CA Trading Co Ltd relating to the potential acquisition of 90% of the equity interest in Can Giuoc Water Works Limited, with provisions for exclusivity, technical and financial due diligence, and a contemplated separate sale and purchase agreement.

On 14 December 2017, following the signing of the Framework Agreement, Thye Kim Meng authorised the remittance by Darco Water Technologies Ltd of a US$1,000,000 deposit to CA Trading Co Ltd as specified in the agreement.
At the material time, Thye Kim Meng worked with finance director Heather Tan and financial controller Teh Chun Sem on matters relating to the Water Project.

On 20 April 2018, a financial due diligence engagement for the Water Project was entered into with Mazars LLP; the engagement was handled by the Investment Committee and finance personnel rather than directly by Thye Kim Meng.

On 6 August 2018, Mazars LLP sent a draft financial due diligence report on the Water Project to financial controller Teh Chun Sem; Thye Kim Meng was not copied on the email correspondence between Mazars LLP and the Investment Committee from 18 April 2018 to 6 August 2018.

In October 2018, after discussing solar power generation as a business opportunity with consultant Dinh Minh Dao, Thye Kim Meng was informed of a joint venture via Con Dao Green Energy to construct and operate a solar power plant on Dat Doc beach, and he identified this as the Solar Project involving a potential acquisition of Con Dao Green Energy.
In October 2018, Thye Kim Meng instructed Darco Water System Sdn Bhd managing director Thye Ze Pin to work on the Solar Project and to lead the team handling due diligence and project execution.

On 30 October 2018, under the direction of group management including Thye Kim Meng, Darco Water System Sdn Bhd entered into a letter of intent with Con Dao Green Energy for the Solar Project, which included a payment schedule and conditions related to legal and project documentation and subsequent agreements.

On 30 November 2018, Darco Water System Sdn Bhd and Con Dao Green Energy signed the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter, which set out a six-tranche payment schedule for an advance sum of US$1,000,000 and conditions for each tranche, including provision of legal agreements, titles, licences, approvals, permits, and completion of due diligence to the satisfaction of Darco Water System Sdn Bhd, as well as refund provisions if specified conditions were not fulfilled.

On 30 November 2018, acting in his capacity as managing director and chief executive officer of Darco Water Technologies Ltd, and together with Darco Water System Sdn Bhd managing director Thye Ze Pin, Thye Kim Meng authorised a first payment of US$200,000 to consultant Dinh Minh Dao under the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter for the Solar Project.

On 10 January 2019, following instructions from Darco Water System Sdn Bhd managing director Thye Ze Pin, Thye Kim Meng authorised a second payment of US$300,000 to consultant Dinh Minh Dao under the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter for the Solar Project.

On 28 December 2018, Thye Kim Meng was copied on an email from finance director Heather Tan to consultant Dinh Minh Dao requesting additional data regarding the salinity of the water source for the Water Project.
On 25 February 2019, at an audit committee meeting of Darco Water Technologies Ltd, Thye Kim Meng stated that the due diligence process for the Water Project was ongoing.

On 14 February 2019, consultant Dinh Minh Dao sent a WhatsApp message referencing an agreement with "Mr Thye" for a payment of US$100,000 for the Solar Project, and financial controller Teh Chun Sem telephoned Thye Kim Meng to seek authorisation for this payment.

On 15 February 2019, after Thye Kim Meng indicated his agreement in principle to the requested payment and Darco Water System Sdn Bhd managing director Thye Ze Pin confirmed that payment could proceed, a third payment of US$100,000 was made to consultant Dinh Minh Dao under the 30 November 2018 Advancement Letter for the Solar Project.
For the Solar Project, Thye Kim Meng approved an inter-company loan of US$1,000,000 from Darco Water Technologies Ltd to its subsidiary Darco Water System Sdn Bhd to fund project-related payments.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a CEO breaches his fiduciary duties or acts negligently by authorizing substantial preliminary deposits and advance payments for overseas projects without formal Board approval and before the completion of comprehensive due diligence.
Party_Details.Rule: A CEO is authorized to execute preliminary agreements and disburse associated refundable deposits without Board approval if the company's internal governance framework (such as a Group Charter) only mandates Board approval for definitive acquisition contracts exceeding a specific financial threshold. Furthermore, a CEO exercises reasonable care and skill by delegating granular due diligence tasks to appropriate corporate committees or subsidiary directors, and is entitled to rely on those subordinates unless there are glaring red flags demanding immediate executive intervention. A bad commercial outcome does not equate to a breach of duty if the initial decisions were made honestly, within authority, and with a rational business purpose.
Party_Details.Application: As the founder and CEO of Darco Water Technologies Ltd, Thye Kim Meng spearheaded the company's expansion into Vietnam. For the 'Water Project', he caused the company to enter into a Framework Agreement and authorized a US$1,000,000 refundable deposit to CA Trading Co Ltd to secure exclusivity while due diligence was conducted. The Board was not formally consulted prior to this execution. He delegated the subsequent financial due diligence (via Mazars LLP) to the company's Investment Committee and finance directors. For the 'Solar Project', he approved a US$1,000,000 inter-company loan to a subsidiary (DWS), whose managing director then executed an Advancement Letter with a local consultant. Thye Kim Meng co-authorized three tranches of payments totaling US$600,000 under this letter. The company alleges that by bypassing the Board, ignoring the internal Group Charter, and authorizing these multi-million dollar disbursements before due diligence was fully satisfied, he acted recklessly and in breach of his duties, causing the unrecovered losses when the projects were eventually abandoned.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the CEO acted entirely within his authority and did not breach any fiduciary or common law duties. The internal Group Charter only required Board approval for final acquisition contracts over S$20 million. The Framework Agreement and Advancement Letter were explicitly preliminary instruments designed to facilitate due diligence and secure commercial exclusivity; they were not definitive acquisitions. Therefore, the CEO was fully authorized to execute them and disburse the associated refundable deposits without a Board mandate. The deposit quantum was deemed commercially rational based on the target valuation. Regarding due diligence, the CEO properly delegated the granular oversight of the Mazars report to the Investment Committee. He was not copied on the day-to-day emails and reasonably believed the process was actively ongoing. The unresolved technical issues did not constitute glaring red flags that required him to instantly demand a refund. For the Solar Project, the CEO correctly relied on the subsidiary's managing director to ensure the conditions for the payment tranches were met before giving his high-level sign-off. Ultimately, the company's decision to withdraw from the projects occurred long after the CEO's departure and was driven by shifting commercial strategies, not by a realization of failed due diligence. The court concluded that the CEO's actions were honest, authorized commercial decisions, and the resulting financial loss was merely an unfortunate business outcome, not an actionable breach of duty.
